role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers is NOT in the 4-digit 5414 top-10 (the cell is dominated by interior 18.4% and graphic 15.4% designers from sibling codes) — for the 6-digit code, hypothesis est. ~30-40% of wage bill
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~30-40% (hypothesis — record is 4-digit and misses the core occupation)
role_hints.note: Concept sketching, CAD surfacing iterations, rendering and CMF documentation are heavily AI-exposed (generative CAD/rendering); physical prototyping, user research in the field, DFM judgment with manufacturers and client workshops persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: mechanical engineers and model-makers in the unlisted tail (17-2141 shows 3.8% at 4-digit; design consultancies carry more), est. ~15-20%
role_hints.note: Simulation setup, tolerance documentation and drawing packages automate; shop prototyping, testing rigs and vendor-facing DFM iteration are physical.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 12.4% at 4-digit; est. ~12-15% here
role_hints.note: Corporate client relationships and creative direction persist; studio ops and reporting automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps 3.7% + 13-1161 Marketing 2.0% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.5%
role_hints.note: Proposal decks, case-study content and schedule/budget tracking automate; enterprise relationship selling persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: portion of 27-1024 Graphic Designers 15.4% and 27-1011 Art Directors 3.0% in the 4-digit cell genuinely present in product studios, est. ~5-8%
role_hints.note: Renderings, animations, packaging graphics and pitch visuals — near-fully automatable production.
Studio admin, invoicing, contract paperwork — near-fully automatable.

source_hints.source: IDSA (Industrial Designers Society of America) publications and salary/practice surveys
source_hints.why: The industrial design professional body — closest source for AI tool adoption and firm-practice benchmarks in product design consultancies.

source_hints.source: Design consultancy trade coverage (Core77, Design Week) on studio pricing and gen-AI impact
source_hints.why: Trade press is the main documented source on how product design studios bill (project fixed fee vs. retainer) and how AI is changing scope.

source_hints.source: Acquisition history of design consultancies by strategics (e.g. Accenture buying design firms, McKinsey-LUNAR precedent)
source_hints.why: The buyer set for design studios has been consulting/tech strategics, not PE roll-ups — this shapes whether an exit multiple uplift exists at all for this code.
Best available proxy for founder age in a founder-branded studio industry; no succession survey is known.

capture_questions: Product design work is sold as fixed-fee project engagements (sometimes retainers) to corporate clients with procurement departments — when AI cuts concept and CAD hours, do studios keep the margin on unchanged fixed fees, or do clients re-scope and rebid projects downward at renewal? Milestone-based fixed fees are the deflation hinge here — name the repricing cycle.
Who owns the client relationship — the studio brand or the founder/creative director whose reputation won the work? Most sub-$10M studios are founder-branded; what does an acquirer actually retain?

Corporate clients already run large in-house design teams (the dominant industry trend for a decade) — does AI accelerate insourcing by making small internal teams sufficient, cutting the addressable outsourced market regardless of studio efficiency?
Is there any gate — regulatory, certification, tooling — between a client and generative design tools, or is the studio's only moat taste, user-research capability and physical prototyping?
terminal_value_question: Industrial design has no license, no statute, and a shrinking outsourced base as corporates insource design — the studio's defensible residue is physical prototyping, field user research and creative reputation. Does mature AI leave enough paid external demand for that residue, or do in-house teams with generative CAD absorb the function entirely, making this a case of AI replacing the operator's product rather than cutting its costs? Answer by tracking the insourcing trend, not studio sentiment.
special_notes: Data-quality flag: labor_share is 0.9177 — implausibly high, likely an artifact of 2024 QCEW wages over 2022 SUSB receipts in a small, fast-changing code (or receipts under-capture). Treat with caution and sanity-check against any independent revenue estimate before using it in V.

Size-class inversion in the record: the 500+ class (30 firms) shows avg revenue of only $9.0M/firm vs. $45.2M for the 100-499 class — likely multinational design arms with modest US-classified receipts. n_band 131 leans on the 20-99 class (85 firms); the true acquirable pool is small.

Role-mix contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 5414 and its top occupations (interior designers 18.4%, graphic designers 15.4%) belong to sibling codes 541410/541430 — 27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers, this code's core occupation, is absent from the top-10. Rebuild the structure; flag it as hypothesis.

No PE roll-up wave exists in industrial design — historic buyers were consultancies and tech strategics acquiring capability (talent), often at prices reflecting acqui-hire logic rather than EBITDA multiples; do not assume a conventional multiple-arbitrage exit without evidence.

Boundary confusion: firms mixing product design with engineering book in 541330; UX/digital product design mostly books in 541511/541512 or 541430; graphic-led brand studios in 541430. Surveys of 'design firms' rarely isolate this small code (n_total 1,610) — check the sample.
